did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated deep learning models using transfer learning with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically InceptionResNetV2 and DenseNet121, to classify five types of facial pigmented lesions (melasma, ephelides, acquired dermal melanocytosis, solar lentigo, and lentigo maligna/lentigo maligna melanoma) for dermatological diagnosis and laser treatment planning.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'Open Access Original Article' and describes a retrospective study where the authors developed and evaluated deep learning models (InceptionResNetV2 and DenseNet121) on a dataset of 432 clinical images. It includes original methods, results (accuracy metrics, AUC values), and comparisons with dermatologists, which constitutes original experimental research rather than a review of prior literature.

title: Deep Learning-Based Classification System for Facial Pigmented Lesions to Aid Laser Treatment Decisions
brief_summary: This study developed and evaluated deep learning models using transfer learning with InceptionResNetV2 and DenseNet121 architectures to classify five types of facial pigmented lesions (melasma, ephelides, acquired dermal melanocytosis, solar lentigo, and lentigo maligna/melanoma) for laser treatment planning. The models were trained on a dataset of 432 high-resolution clinical images collected retrospectively from Kindai University Hospital. Diagnostic accuracy was evaluated by comparing the AI models against nine board-certified dermatologists and 11 non-certified dermatologists. The InceptionResNetV2 and DenseNet121 models achieved overall diagnostic accuracies of 87% and 86%, respectively, outperforming the median accuracies of expert (80%) and non-expert (63%) dermatologists. Notably, both models achieved 100% sensitivity in identifying lentigo maligna/lentigo maligna melanoma, demonstrating superior performance and potential clinical utility in aiding dermatological diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the five classes in the five-class classification task. 102 images from 102 patients were included. Diagnosed clinically and confirmed by five experts.

all_task_types.target: Five classes of facial pigmented lesions: melasma, ephelides, acquired dermal melanocytosis (ADM), solar lentigo, and lentigo maligna/lentigo maligna melanoma (LM/LMM).
all_task_types.details: The study performs a five-class discrimination task using deep learning models (InceptionResNetV2 and DenseNet121) to classify clinical images of facial pigmented lesions. The models output discrete diagnostic categories. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1 score, and Area Under the Curve (AUC) for each class.

datasets_used.name: Kindai University Hospital Clinical Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A retrospective collection of 432 high-resolution clinical photographs of facial pigmented lesions collected from Kindai University Hospital, Osaka, Japan, between September 6, 2024, and December 31, 2024. Images were anonymized and underwent preprocessing including white balance correction and region of interest (ROI) extraction.

use.training: Used for training the InceptionResNetV2 and DenseNet121 models via transfer learning (80% of images).
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (20% of images).
use.other: The dataset was also used for external comparison against diagnostic accuracies of 9 board-certified dermatologists (experts) and 11 non-certified dermatologists (non-experts).

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantitatively compare the diagnostic performance of InceptionResNetV2 and DenseNet121 models across the five classes of facial pigmented lesions.
To measure the discriminative ability of the models for each lesion class.
To compare the performance of AI models against expert (board-certified) and non-expert dermatologists.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance comparisons between AI models and dermatologists were made using descriptive statistics (median diagnostic accuracy) and ROC curve analysis (AUC values) without statistical significance testing.

Kindai University Hospital Clinical Imag.experiment: Five-class classification of facial pigmented lesions (Melasma, Ephelides, ADM, Solar Lentigo, LM/LMM)
Kindai University Hospital Clinical Imag.notes: Both AI models outperformed the median diagnostic accuracies of expert (80%) and non-expert (63%) dermatologists. Both models achieved 100% sensitivity for LM/LMM. InceptionResNetV2 showed slightly higher AUC for ephelides and melasma compared to DenseNet121.

architecture_used.input_size: Square ROI (dimensions not explicitly specified, but cropped to square to match CNN input requirements)

architecture_details.transfer_learning_strategy: Transfer learning with ImageNet pre-trained weights
architecture_details.frozen_layers: First 10 layers of InceptionResNetV2 or first 100 layers of DenseNet121 were frozen
architecture_details.added_layers: Several dense layers and GlobalAveragePooling2D layers added
architecture_details.regularization: L2 regularization and dropout applied to added layers

data_augmentation: Custom preprocessing: adjustments in hue, saturation, and brightness

classification_or_output_head.details: Top layer excluded from pre-trained model; new output layer added for five classes (melasma, ephelides, acquired dermal melanocytosis, solar lentigo, and lentigo maligna/lentigo maligna melanoma)

preprocessing_techniques: White balance correction using Python's OpenCV to normalize color representation and reduce illumination variability
Region of Interest (ROI) extraction: Cropping a square ROI containing the pigmented lesion to match CNN input requirements
Quality control via image blurriness evaluation: Assessment of image clarity using Laplacian-based variance on grayscale images; only images with variance ≥ 10 were retained
Rescaling: Normalizing pixel values from 0 to 1
Data augmentation: Rotation, shift, shear, zoom, and horizontal flipping
Custom preprocessing adjustments: Modifications to hue, saturation, and brightness
Edge detection enhancement: Application of a Sobel filter

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Geometric transform applied via ImageDataGenerator; specific angles or probabilities not reported.
Geometric transform applied via ImageDataGenerator; specific ranges or probabilities not reported.
Geometric transform applied via ImageDataGenerator; specific angles or probabilities not reported.

Geometric transform applied via ImageDataGenerator; probability not reported.
Adjustments in hue, saturation, and brightness applied to image data.
Application of a Sobel filter to enhance edge detection.

conclusions: The developed deep learning models (InceptionResNetV2 and DenseNet121) demonstrated superior diagnostic performance compared to both expert (median accuracy 80%) and non-expert (median accuracy 63%) dermatologists in differentiating among five types of facial pigmented lesions.
InceptionResNetV2 and DenseNet121 achieved overall diagnostic accuracies of 87% and 86%, respectively.
Both AI models achieved 100% sensitivity in identifying lentigo maligna/lentigo maligna melanoma (LM/LMM).
InceptionResNetV2 exhibited slightly higher AUC values for ephelides and melasma compared to DenseNet121.

The findings suggest that these deep learning models have potential clinical applicability in assisting dermatological diagnosis and guiding appropriate treatment strategies, particularly for laser treatment planning.
important_remarks: Data leakage risk: The dataset was split at the image level rather than the patient level, meaning images from the same patient could exist in both training and validation sets, likely leading to an overestimation of the model's generalization performance.

Limited generalizability due to small sample size: The study used a relatively small dataset of 432 images collected from a single center (Kindai University Hospital) over a short period (Sept-Dec 2024), which may limit applicability to broader, more diverse populations.

Lack of external validation: The study relied on a single internal split and did not perform external validation or k-fold cross-validation with patient-level splitting, which is necessary to robustly assess model stability.

Preprocessing dependency: The model relied on specific preprocessing steps (white balance correction, ROI extraction, and blurriness filtering) that may not be consistently reproducible in real-world, uncontrolled clinical environments where automatic correction might vary.

Narrow lesion scope: The model was trained only on five specific lesion types, excluding other common facial pigmented lesions (e.g., post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation) and rare conditions, limiting its broader diagnostic utility.

No statistical significance testing: The comparison between AI models and dermatologists was based on descriptive statistics (median accuracy) without reported p-values or formal hypothesis testing to confirm the statistical significance of the performance differences.
Limited clinical scope: The system is designed strictly for diagnostic differentiation to aid laser treatment planning and does not predict treatment outcomes or select specific laser parameters.
